title: Aston Villa F.C.

infobox.value: [V Sports](/wiki/V_Sports "V Sports") ([Nassef Sawiris](/wiki/Nassef_Sawiris "Nassef Sawiris"), [Wes Edens](/wiki/Wes_Edens "Wes Edens") & Atairos)

is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Aston](/wiki/Aston "Aston"), [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England. It was founded in 1874. Its team compete in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), the top tier of [English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"), and have played at their home ground, [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"), since [1897](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1896–97 Aston Villa F.C. season"). Aston Villa is one of the oldest and most successful clubs in England, having won the [Football League First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") seven times, the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") seven times, the [League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup") five times, and the [European Cup](/wiki/European_Cup "European Cup") and [European Super Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Super_Cup "UEFA Super Cup") once.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Association football club in Birmingham, England

For the women's team, see [Aston Villa W.F.C.](/wiki/Aston_Villa_W.F.C. "Aston Villa W.F.C.") For their youth teams, see [Aston Villa F.C. Under-21s and Academy](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C._Under-21s_and_Academy "Aston Villa F.C. Under-21s and Academy").

**Aston Villa Football Club** is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Aston](/wiki/Aston "Aston"), [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England. It was founded in 1874. Its team compete in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), the top tier of [English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"), and have played at their home ground, [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"), since [1897](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1896–97 Aston Villa F.C. season"). Aston Villa is one of the oldest and most successful clubs in England, having won the [Football League First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") seven times, the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") seven times, the [League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup") five times, and the [European Cup](/wiki/European_Cup "European Cup") and [European Super Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Super_Cup "UEFA Super Cup") once.

Aston Villa has been a leading English club since the 1880s, when it was a pioneer of the modern passing game. This short, slick combination passing style was introduced by Scotsman [George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)"), who was appointed as the world's first professional football manager in 1886. The club was influential in the sport's move to professionalism in 1885, and it was a Villa director, [William McGregor](/wiki/William_McGregor_(football) "William McGregor (football)"), who founded the world's first Football League [in 1888](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1888–89 Aston Villa F.C. season").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

George Ramsay's trophy haul of six League Championships and six FA Cups established Aston Villa as the most successful club in England, a position it held from the 1890s until the 1970s. Villa scored 128 goals in [season 1930–31](/wiki/1930-31_avfc "1930-31 avfc"), which remains the all-time top-flight record,[[7]](#cite_note-7) however the club began its first decline in the mid-1930s; the 1940s and 1950s were generally a period of mediocrity followed by a steep decline in the 1960s which culminated in a takeover of the club by [Doug Ellis](/wiki/Doug_Ellis "Doug Ellis") in 1968 and Villa's first and only relegation to the third tier of English football in 1969–70. Villa returned to the elite from the mid-1970s under manager [Ron Saunders](/wiki/Ron_Saunders "Ron Saunders"), who led the club to a seventh top-flight league title in 1980–81. They became only the fourth English club to win the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), in [1981–82](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_European_Cup "1981–82 European Cup"), followed by the [European Super Cup](/wiki/European_Super_Cup "European Super Cup") in [1982](/wiki/1982_European_Super_Cup "1982 European Super Cup").

Aston Villa were a founding member of the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") in [1992](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1992–93 Aston Villa F.C. season"), one of just three clubs to have been a founding member of both the Football League and the Premier League. Throughout the 1990s and early 2000's, Villa regularly qualified for European football, but following a period of decline after the departure of [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill") the club suffered its only relegation from the Premier League in [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "2015–16 Aston Villa F.C. season"). After three seasons in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") and two ownership changes the club returned to the Premier League in 2019. Under [Unai Emery](/wiki/Unai_Emery "Unai Emery") in 2024, the club qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for the first time since [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1982–83 Aston Villa F.C. season").

During their history Villa has spent 112 seasons in the top-flight, the second highest of any club, and provided 78 England internationals, also the second highest of any club. Aston Villa is ranked fifth in the all-time English top flight table, since its creation in [1888](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_Football_League "1888–89 Football League")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and is the [seventh most successful club](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_England_by_competitive_honours_won "List of football clubs in England by competitive honours won") in English football by competitive honours.

Villa have a fierce local rivalry with [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and the [Second City derby](/wiki/Second_City_derby "Second City derby") between the teams has been played [since 1879](/wiki/1879-1880_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1879-1880 Aston Villa F.C. season").[[9]](#cite_note-Derby-9) There is also a local rivalry with [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), with matches between the sides known as the [West Midlands derby](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C.%E2%80%93West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C._rivalry "Aston Villa F.C.–West Bromwich Albion F.C. rivalry"). The club's traditional [kit](/wiki/Kit_(association_football) "Kit (association football)") colours are [claret](/wiki/Wine_(color)#Claret "Wine (color)") shirts with sky blue sleeves, white shorts and sky blue socks. Their traditional club badge is of a [rampant](/wiki/Attitude_(heraldry)#Rampant "Attitude (heraldry)") lion.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-introducing-11) The club is currently owned by [V Sports](/wiki/V_Sports "V Sports"), a company owned by the Egyptian billionaire [Nassef Sawiris](/wiki/Nassef_Sawiris "Nassef Sawiris"), the American billionaire [Wes Edens](/wiki/Wes_Edens "Wes Edens"), and American investment company Atairos.

## History

For more details on this topic, see [History of Aston Villa F.C. (1874–1961)](/wiki/History_of_Aston_Villa_F.C._(1874%E2%80%931961) "History of Aston Villa F.C. (1874–1961)") and [History of Aston Villa F.C. (1961–present)](/wiki/History_of_Aston_Villa_F.C._(1961%E2%80%93present) "History of Aston Villa F.C. (1961–present)").

### Formation and rise to prominence (1874−1886)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/George_Ramsay.jpg/250px-George_Ramsay.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Ramsay.jpg)

[George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)")'s trophy haul of six League Championships and six FA Cups established Aston Villa as the most successful club in England. He has been described as the world's first paid football manager.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Mcgregor.jpg/250px-Mcgregor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mcgregor.jpg)

[William McGregor](/wiki/William_McGregor_(football) "William McGregor (football)") (1846–1911), founder of The Football League

Aston Villa are believed to have formed [on 21 November 1874](/wiki/1873-74_in_English_football "1873-74 in English football"), by members of the Aston Villa [Wesleyan](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") Chapel in [Handsworth](/wiki/Handsworth,_West_Midlands "Handsworth, West Midlands"): which is now part of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The chapel stood at Villa Cross, a road junction whose name derived from a house known as *Aston Villa*, a suburban [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") villa that over time had given its name to the surrounding locality.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The four founders of Aston Villa Football Club were Jack Hughes, Frederick Matthews, Walter Price and William Scattergood, who were members of the chapel's cricket team looking for a way to stay fit during the winter months. Having watched a fellow member of their Bible class playing in a rugby match, they paused beneath a dim gas lamp near Villa Cross and agreed that rugby was a little too rough, deciding instead to play association football.[[14]](#cite_note-SM1-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The meeting beneath the gas lamp is traditionally regarded as the birth of Aston Villa F.C.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Due to the lack of local football teams Aston Villa's first match was against the local [Aston Brook St Mary's](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Aston_Brook "St Mary's Church, Aston Brook") [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") team. As a condition of the match, the Villa side had to agree to play the first half under Rugby rules and the second half under Association rules. Villa won their first game 1–0.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The infant club's fortunes changed forever when a young Scotsman called [George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)") stumbled across the Villa players' practice match in [Aston Park](/wiki/Aston_Park,_Birmingham "Aston Park, Birmingham") in 1876. He was asked to make up the numbers, and they were amazed by his skills; they had never seen such a display of close ball control before. When the game was over, the Villa players surrounded him and invited him to join the club and become their captain.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Word spread about how fine a player Ramsay was, spectators began turning up to watch the little man nicknamed ‘Scotty’. He also took charge of training, Ramsay later described the newly formed club's approach to the game as 'a dash at the man and a big kick at the ball'.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
Ramsay, on the other hand, had been influenced by the Scottish club [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_FC "Queen's Park FC"), who pioneered what became known as 'combination football' in his native [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"). The intricate passing game he introduced was a revolutionary move for an English club in the late 1870s.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Villa began to establish themselves as one of the best teams in the Midlands, winning their first honour, the [Birmingham Senior Cup](/wiki/Birmingham_Senior_Cup "Birmingham Senior Cup") [in 1880](/wiki/1879%E2%80%9380_AVFC_season "1879–80 AVFC season"). The club went on to lift the trophy nine times in the next 12 seasons.[[22]](#cite_note-Hall_of_Fame-22)

### Victorian and Edwardian golden age (1886–1914)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/AstonVilla1896-97.jpg/250px-AstonVilla1896-97.jpg)](/wiki/File:AstonVilla1896-97.jpg)

The Aston Villa [Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") winning team of 1896–97 with the [First Division Championship](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") and the [FA Cup](/wiki/1897_FA_Cup_final "1897 FA Cup final")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Villa_Champions_1899.jpg/250px-Villa_Champions_1899.jpg)](/wiki/File:Villa_Champions_1899.jpg)

The Aston Villa team of 1899 that won the First Division and [Sheriff of London Charity Shield](/wiki/Sheriff_of_London_Charity_Shield "Sheriff of London Charity Shield") (shared with Queen's Park), as well as a number of [county cup honours](/wiki/County_football_association "County football association")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/FACupFinal1905NewcastleVilla.jpg/250px-FACupFinal1905NewcastleVilla.jpg)](/wiki/File:FACupFinal1905NewcastleVilla.jpg)

[Harry Hampton](/wiki/Harry_Hampton_(footballer,_born_1885) "Harry Hampton (footballer, born 1885)") scores one of his two goals in the [1905 FA Cup final](/wiki/1905_FA_Cup_final "1905 FA Cup final").

Following the professionalisation of football in 1885, the club decided that it needed a full-time paid manager. The following advert was placed in the [Birmingham Daily Gazette](/wiki/Birmingham_Gazette "Birmingham Gazette") newspaper in June 1886:

> 'Wanted: manager for Aston Villa Football Club, who will be required to devote his whole time under direction of the committee. Salary £100 per annum. Applications with reference must be made not later than June 23rd to Chairman of the Committee, Aston Villa Club House, 6 Witton Road, Aston’

Villa received 150 applicants for the role, but with his strong association with the club George Ramsay was the overwhelming choice of the membership. Thus on 26 June 1886, Aston Villa appointed what has been described as the world's first professional football manager.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

During the following season Aston Villa rose to national prominence, as the first Midlands team to win the FA Cup in 1887. Villa's captain, the powerful Scottish centre-forward [Archie Hunter](/wiki/Archie_Hunter "Archie Hunter"), became one of the game's first household names, being the first player to score in every round of the FA Cup. Aston Villa were one of the dozen teams that competed in the inaugural [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") in 1888 with one of the club's directors, [William McGregor](/wiki/William_McGregor_(football) "William McGregor (football)") being the league's founder. Following the professionalisation of football in 1885, clubs needed regular income to pay their players' wages. Frequently [friendlies](/wiki/Exhibition_match "Exhibition match") were cancelled due to opponents' FA Cup or [county cup](/wiki/County_cup "County cup") matches or clubs simply failed to honour a fixture in favour of a more lucrative match elsewhere.[[24]](#cite_note-PS1-24)[[25]](#cite_note-HD-25) McGregor took action after seeing Villa matches cancelled, to the increasing frustration of the club's fans, on five consecutive Saturdays.[[25]](#cite_note-HD-25) In March 1888, he wrote to the committee of his own club, Aston Villa, as well as to those of [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C."), [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C."), [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."),[[26]](#cite_note-26) suggesting the creation of a league competition that would provide a number of guaranteed fixtures for its member clubs each season.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Following two meetings between representatives of the leading clubs, the world's first Football League season began in September 1888 with 12 member clubs from the Midlands and north of England: [Accrington](/wiki/Accrington_F.C. "Accrington F.C."), Aston Villa, Blackburn Rovers, Bolton Wanderers, [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."), [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."), [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C."), [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), Preston North End, [Stoke](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers.

Despite Villa founding the league, by 1893 they had yet to win it. Villa Committee Member [Frederick Rinder](/wiki/Frederick_Rinder "Frederick Rinder") was the instigator of a club meeting at Barwick Street in February 1893 that removed the committee running the club at the time. All fourteen committee members resigned and were replaced by a committee of five led by Rinder after he gave a rousing speech criticising the board's tolerance of ill discipline and players' drinking. On the pitch, manager [George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)") was moulding a team that became renowned for its short, quick combination passing which saw Villa win its first league title in [1893–94](/wiki/1893-94_Football_League "1893-94 Football League"); the season after that the club won its second FA Cup in [1894-95](/wiki/1895_FA_Cup_Final "1895 FA Cup Final"). This was followed by back-to-back League titles in [1895–96](/wiki/1895-96_Football_League "1895-96 Football League") and [1896–97](/wiki/1896-97_Football_League "1896-97 Football League").

Aston Villa emerged as the most successful English club of the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), winning no fewer than five League titles and three FA Cups by the end of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")'s reign in 1901.[[29]](#cite_note-Ward192-29) Villa's captain during this era was Birmingham-born forward [John Devey](/wiki/John_Devey "John Devey"), who enjoyed a successful partnership with the lightning-fast winger [Charlie Athersmith](/wiki/Charlie_Athersmith "Charlie Athersmith") and marshalling Villa's defence was the tough-tackling Scotsman [James Cowan](/wiki/James_Cowan_(footballer) "James Cowan (footballer)"), who had an unrivalled sense of timing and anticipation.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [In 1897](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "1896–97 Aston Villa F.C. season"), the year Villa won [The Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)"), they moved into their present home, the Aston Lower Grounds.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Supporters coined the name "Villa Park"; no official declaration listed the ground as [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Success continued into the [Edwardian era](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era"), with Villa lifting the FA Cup for the fourth time in [1904–05](/wiki/1905_FA_Cup_Final "1905 FA Cup Final"), and a sixth league title in [1909–10](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Football_League "1909–10 Football League"). A further FA Cup triumph was achieved on the eve of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1913, with the club narrowly missing out on winning a second [Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)"), finishing runners-up in the league. Star players during this era included [Howard Spencer](/wiki/Howard_Spencer "Howard Spencer"), the cultured defender who captained both Villa and England, and the prolific strike force of [Joe Bache](/wiki/Joe_Bache "Joe Bache") and [Harry Hampton](/wiki/Harry_Hampton_(footballer,_born_1885) "Harry Hampton (footballer, born 1885)") who between them scored 382 goals in claret and blue.

### Relative decline and first relegation (1920–1939)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Billy_Walker_Footballer.png/250px-Billy_Walker_Footballer.png)](/wiki/File:Billy_Walker_Footballer.png)

A one-club man, [Billy Walker](/wiki/Billy_Walker_(footballer,_born_1897) "Billy Walker (footballer, born 1897)") (1897–1964) scored 244 goals in 531 appearances for Villa between 1920 and 1934. He is Aston Villa's all-time top goalscorer.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Tom_%27Pongo%27_Waring.jpg/250px-Tom_%27Pongo%27_Waring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tom_%27Pongo%27_Waring.jpg)

[Tom 'Pongo' Waring](/wiki/Tom_%27Pongo%27_Waring "Tom 'Pongo' Waring") (1906–1980) scored 50 goals for Villa in the 1930–31 season, a record-breaking campaign in which the team scored 128 top-flight goals.

In January 1920, [Billy Walker](/wiki/Billy_Walker_(footballer,_born_1897) "Billy Walker (footballer, born 1897)") scored twice on his Villa debut in a 2–1 FA Cup first-round win over [QPR](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers "Queens Park Rangers"); the club won the FA Cup for the sixth time that season and Walker went on to establish himself as Villa's star player of the 1920s, scoring a record 244 goals in 531 appearances, captaining Villa and [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"). [George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)") retired in 1926, at the age of 71, and his replacement [Billy Smith](/wiki/W._J._Smith "W. J. Smith") was unable to continue Ramsay's success. In reality, several other football clubs had caught up with Aston Villa, most notably [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_FC "Arsenal FC"), to which the club finished runners-up in the league in [1930–31](/wiki/1930%E2%80%9331_Football_League "1930–31 Football League") and [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Football_League "1932–33 Football League"). Despite missing out on the league title, Villa Park crowds were entertained by attacking football; the 128 goals scored in the 1930–31 season remains the all-time top-flight record. A remarkable 49 of the league goals that season were scored by centre-forward [Tom 'Pongo' Waring](/wiki/Tom_%27Pongo%27_Waring "Tom 'Pongo' Waring"), with another 30 goals from winger [Eric Houghton](/wiki/Eric_Houghton "Eric Houghton").

The club appointed [Jimmy McMullan](/wiki/Jimmy_McMullan "Jimmy McMullan") as manager in 1934, however, the move proved disastrous, resulting in Villa's first ever relegation in 1935–36 after 48 years in the top flight. Villa struggled largely due to a dismal defensive record: they conceded 110 goals in 42 games, 7 of them coming from [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")'s [Ted Drake](/wiki/Ted_Drake "Ted Drake") in an [infamous 1–7 defeat](/wiki/Aston_Villa_1%E2%80%937_Arsenal_(14_December_1935) "Aston Villa 1–7 Arsenal (14 December 1935)") at Villa Park.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The club made seven signings and spent a staggering sum for the time of £35,500 trying to retain top-flight status at all costs, but were unable to buy their way out of trouble. Aston Villa, at the time one of the most famous and successful clubs in world football, was relegated in 1936 for the first time in its history.

Following relegation to the Second Division, the Villa board brought back the ageing former club chairman [Fred Rinder](/wiki/Frederick_Rinder "Frederick Rinder"), who said on his return "Villa have been a great club, are still a great club, and always will be a great club". He was vocal in his criticism of the board for its "almost total neglect of the reserve team, instead relying on paying big fees for ready made players". He believed that this change in policy from scouting and developing young homegrown talent led to a decline in the club's culture and style of play, which alongside a tolerance of ill-discipline in the players led to Villa's relegation. Rinder's first act was to travel to Austria to recruit the progressive coach [Jimmy Hogan](/wiki/Jimmy_Hogan "Jimmy Hogan") as manager. Within two seasons, Hogan had guided Villa back to the top flight as Second Division champions playing attractive free-flowing football. Hogan outlined his philosophy: "I am a teacher and lover of constructive football with every pass, every kick, every movement an object."[[34]](#cite_note-34) He used to tell his players that "football was like a [Viennese waltz](/wiki/Viennese_waltz "Viennese waltz"), a rhapsody. One-two-three, one-two-three, pass-move-pass, pass-move-pass."[[35]](#cite_note-:4-35) However, the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") ended Hogan's project to restore Aston Villa to the top of the English game.

### Mediocrity and discontent (1945–1961)

Like all English clubs, Villa lost seven seasons to the Second World War, and that conflict brought several careers to a premature end.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Bumper crowds flocked to Villa Park following the war, 76,588 people attended the FA Cup quarter-final between Villa and [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County "Derby County") in March 1946, which is the all-time record attendance at Villa Park. The team was rebuilt under the guidance of former player [Alex Massie](/wiki/Alex_Massie_(footballer) "Alex Massie (footballer)") for the remainder of the 1940s. Star players of this era included the one-club man [Harry Parkes](/wiki/Harry_Parkes_(footballer,_born_1920) "Harry Parkes (footballer, born 1920)"), the Welsh centre forward [Trevor Ford](/wiki/Trevor_Ford "Trevor Ford") and inside-forward [Johnny Dixon](/wiki/Johnny_Dixon_(footballer) "Johnny Dixon (footballer)"), however the club only achieved mid-table finishes throughout the forties and fifties, never finishing higher than 6th place in the league. The board came in for increasing criticism during this time, with the 1953 AGM described by the [Sports Argus](/wiki/Sports_Argus "Sports Argus") as "the longest and liveliest Villa meeting".[[37]](#cite_note-37) Shareholders and supporters criticised the club's lack of youth development, recruitment and training methods. When [Danny Blanchflower](/wiki/Danny_Blanchflower "Danny Blanchflower") submitted a transfer request in 1954 he said that "the club had grown fat and lazy on its old traditions and the decay was eating at the once solid foundations".[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Despite narrowly avoiding relegation the season before, Aston Villa's first trophy for 37 years came in the 1956–57 season when another former Villa player, [Eric Houghton](/wiki/Eric_Houghton "Eric Houghton") led the club to a then record seventh FA Cup Final win, defeating the 'Busby Babes' of [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") 2–1 with Northern Irish winger [Peter McParland](/wiki/Peter_McParland "Peter McParland") scoring both goals.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The team continued to struggle for consistency in the league though, which led to Eric Houghton being sacked in December 1958. His replacement [Joe Mercer](/wiki/Joe_Mercer "Joe Mercer") could not prevent the club being relegated for only the second time in their history in [1958-59](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Football_League "1958–59 Football League"). However, under the stewardship of Mercer, Villa returned to the top-flight in 1960 as Second Division Champions with a talented young side which became known as 'Mercer's Minors'. In the following season Aston Villa became the first team to win the [Football League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup"), with England centre-forward [Gerry Hitchens](/wiki/Gerry_Hitchens "Gerry Hitchens") scoring an impressive 42 goals in [1960-61](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_Football_League "1960–61 Football League").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Deep malaise and revival (1961–1974)

Hitchens' goals brought him to the attention of Italian club [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), who offered him a large financial incentive to sign. He was sold for £85,000 in summer of 1961, his replacement, [Derek Dougan](/wiki/Derek_Dougan "Derek Dougan") was not a success and Villa slid backwards. Mercer's forced retirement from the club in July 1964, following a stress-induced stroke, signalled a period of deep turmoil and malaise. The most successful club in England had failed to keep pace with changes in the modern game; three of the five-man board of directors were over 70 years old, the club had neglected its scouting network and coaching structure and the club's finances were in a parlous state. This led to the club selling its top striker [Tony Hateley](/wiki/Tony_Hateley "Tony Hateley") to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") for £100,000 in October 1966, without his goals Villa were relegated for the third time in its history, under manager [Dick Taylor](/wiki/Dick_Taylor_(football_manager) "Dick Taylor (football manager)") in 1967. The board even sold the club's training ground outside Villa Park for housing, leaving the team in the position of training on borrowed training pitches of local factory teams.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The following season the fans called for the board to resign as Villa finished 16th in the Second Division. With mounting debts and Villa lying at the bottom of Division Two, the board sacked [Tommy Cummings](/wiki/Tommy_Cummings "Tommy Cummings") (the manager brought in to replace Taylor), and within weeks the entire board resigned under overwhelming pressure from fans.[[42]](#cite_note-Ward,_Adam;_Griffin,_Jeremy;_p.100-42) After much speculation, control of the club was bought by London financier Pat Matthews, who brought in [Doug Ellis](/wiki/Doug_Ellis "Doug Ellis") as chairman in December 1968.[[42]](#cite_note-Ward,_Adam;_Griffin,_Jeremy;_p.100-42) Ellis later recalled that "you could write your name in the dust, window frames were rotting, the smell of failure and imminent financial ruin hung in the air"; one of their first acts was to raise £205,835 in a share issue which cleared the club's debts. Doug Ellis's first managerial appointment was the outspoken Scottish manager [Tommy Docherty](/wiki/Tommy_Docherty "Tommy Docherty"), who after initial success, was sacked after 13 months in charge with the club at the foot of Second Division. His replacement was former club captain and reserve team manager [Vic Crowe](/wiki/Vic_Crowe "Vic Crowe"), who could not prevent Villa being relegated to the Third Division for the first time in its history at the end of the [1969–70](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_in_English_football "1969–70 in English football") season.

The following season Villa surprised everyone by beating [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") in the two-legged semi-final to reach the [1971 League Cup Final](/wiki/1971_Football_League_Cup_Final "1971 Football League Cup Final"), in which the team played well but were defeated by two late [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") goals. There was a renewed sense of optimism at Villa Park as the club was promoted to the Second Division as champions with average attendances of 30,000 and a record 70 points in [1971–72 season](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_in_English_football "1971–72 in English football").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Off the pitch, the board purchased the new 20-acre [Bodymoor Heath Training Ground](/wiki/Bodymoor_Heath_Training_Ground "Bodymoor Heath Training Ground") in December 1971, with a view to improving the club's youth development and coaching facilities.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Back among the elite (1974–1992)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Dennis_Mortimer_%281982%29.jpg/250px-Dennis_Mortimer_%281982%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dennis_Mortimer_(1982).jpg)

Aston Villa captain Dennis Mortimer lifted the European Cup in 1982.

Following a 14th-place finish in the Second Division, Crowe was replaced in August 1974 by [Ron Saunders](/wiki/Ron_Saunders "Ron Saunders"). He was a fitness fanatic, whose brand of no-nonsense man-management proved effective, with the club winning the League Cup the following season and, by the end of season 1974–75, he had taken Aston Villa back into the First Division and into Europe.[[45]](#cite_note-45) One player who had been a mainstay of the Villa team throughout the rollercoaster of relegations and subsequent revival was fan-favourite [Charlie Aitkin](/wiki/Charlie_Aitken_(footballer,_born_1942) "Charlie Aitken (footballer, born 1942)"), who made 659 appearances at [left back](/wiki/Left_back "Left back") for the club between 1959 and 1976, making him Villa's all-time record appearance holder.

Aston Villa were back among the elite as Saunders continued to mould a winning team, finishing 4th in the league and winning a further League Cup in [1976–77](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_in_English_football "1976–77 in English football"), with the formidable strike partnership of [Brian Little](/wiki/Brian_Little "Brian Little") and [Andy Gray](/wiki/Andy_Gray_(footballer,_born_1955) "Andy Gray (footballer, born 1955)"), who became the first player to win both the [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year") and [PFA Players' Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Players%27_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Players' Player of the Year") in the same season.

The 1970s was an era of boardroom unrest at Villa Park. Ron Saunders had a strained relationship with [Doug Ellis](/wiki/Doug_Ellis "Doug Ellis"), resenting Doug's perceived interference in football matters. Over time Ellis became an isolated figure on the board, as the other directors sided with Saunders. He was ousted as chairman in 1975 to make way for [Sir William Dugdale](/wiki/Sir_William_Dugdale,_2nd_Baronet "Sir William Dugdale, 2nd Baronet"). He remained on the board until 1979, when he left the club after a protracted power struggle with majority shareholder [Ron Bendall](/wiki/Ron_Bendall "Ron Bendall"). With Ellis gone, Saunders became all-powerful as manager.

Villa achieved a seventh top-flight league title in [1980–81](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_in_English_football "1980–81 in English football"), with players such as [Gordon Cowans](/wiki/Gordon_Cowans "Gordon Cowans"), [Tony Morley](/wiki/Tony_Morley "Tony Morley") and captain [Dennis Mortimer](/wiki/Dennis_Mortimer "Dennis Mortimer") leading the club to its first top-flight title in 71 years. Remarkably, they did so using just 14 players, with seven players being ever-presents. Villa's Birmingham-born forward [Gary Shaw](/wiki/Gary_Shaw_(footballer,_born_1961) "Gary Shaw (footballer, born 1961)") was named 1980-1981 [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year").

To the surprise of commentators and fans, Ron Saunders quit halfway through the [1981–82](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_in_English_football "1981–82 in English football") season, with Villa in the quarter final of the European Cup. Saunders had expressed his exasperation with the board at the lack of funds available to him to strengthen the team and fell out with the chairman Ron Bendall over the terms of his contract.[[46]](#cite_note-46) He was replaced by his softly-spoken assistant manager [Tony Barton](/wiki/Tony_Barton_(footballer) "Tony Barton (footballer)") who guided the club to a 1–0 victory over [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") in the [European Cup final](/wiki/1982_European_Cup_Final "1982 European Cup Final") in [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam") courtesy of a [Peter Withe](/wiki/Peter_Withe "Peter Withe") goal in the 67th minute. Ten minutes into the final, Villa's first choice goalkeeper, [Jimmy Rimmer](/wiki/Jimmy_Rimmer "Jimmy Rimmer"), was injured and young substitute keeper [Nigel Spink](/wiki/Nigel_Spink "Nigel Spink") was called into action, having only made one previous appearance in the first team. Spink performed superbly, keeping a clean sheet, and helping Villa become only the fourth English club to lift the European Cup.

[![In the foreground is two men holding a large cup, they have claret scarves and a medal around their necks. Around them are ten old players in suits with medals and scarves around their necks](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/82team.jpg/250px-82team.jpg)](/wiki/File:82team.jpg)

The 1982 European Cup winning squad celebrate the 25th anniversary of their win.

The following season the defence of the European Cup ended in a quarter-final defeat to [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus"), but Villa won the [European Super Cup](/wiki/1982_European_Super_Cup "1982 European Super Cup"), beating [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") 3–1 on aggregate. This marked a pinnacle though and Villa's fortunes declined sharply for most of the 1980s. Doug Ellis returned as chairman and majority shareholder in November 1982. The club was saddled with significant debts and questions had been raised by the police regarding fraudulent financial activity surrounding the building of the North Stand at Villa Park from 1980 to 1982. The cost of the work was £1.3 million. An internal investigation found that £700,000 of the £1.3 million worth of bills were unaccounted for.[[47]](#cite_note-Hayes172-47) A later report by accountants [Deloitte](/wiki/Deloitte "Deloitte") found that there were "serious breaches of recommended codes of practice and poor site supervision".[[48]](#cite_note-48) Ellis immediately set about trying to reduce the club's overheads. He informed the players that they needed to take pay cuts and told the manager Tony Barton that there was a need to reduce the playing staff.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Saunders' team was broken up and not adequately replaced, culminating in the club being relegated in 1987, just five years after Villa had been crowned European champions.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

However, Villa bounced back quickly, achieving promotion the following year under [Graham Taylor](/wiki/Graham_Taylor_(footballer) "Graham Taylor (footballer)") and a runners-up position in the top-flight in the 1989–90 season with a fine side that included [Paul McGrath](/wiki/Paul_McGrath_(footballer) "Paul McGrath (footballer)"), [Tony Daley](/wiki/Tony_Daley "Tony Daley") and [David Platt](/wiki/David_Platt_(footballer) "David Platt (footballer)").[[51]](#cite_note-51) Following this success, Graham Taylor accepted the offer to take over as England manager in 1990.

### 24 years in the Premier League (1992–2016)

Villa were one of the founding members of the Premier League in 1992, one of just three clubs to have been founding members of both the Football League in 1888 and the Premier League, along with [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers "Blackburn Rovers") and [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C."). Villa finished runners-up to Manchester United in the inaugural season under manager [Ron Atkinson](/wiki/Ron_Atkinson "Ron Atkinson"). His side lifted the League Cup in 1994, beating Manchester United 3–1 in the final, with goals from [Dalian Atkinson](/wiki/Dalian_Atkinson "Dalian Atkinson") and [Dean Saunders](/wiki/Dean_Saunders "Dean Saunders"), but the team struggled for form in the league and Atkinson was replaced by former Villa striker [Brian Little](/wiki/Brian_Little "Brian Little") in November 1994. Little assembled a young side which included players as [Gareth Southgate](/wiki/Gareth_Southgate "Gareth Southgate"), [Steve Staunton](/wiki/Steve_Staunton "Steve Staunton"), [Ian Taylor](/wiki/Ian_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1968) "Ian Taylor (footballer, born 1968)") and [Dwight Yorke](/wiki/Dwight_Yorke "Dwight Yorke"), leading the club to a fifth League Cup triumph in 1996, beating [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United "Leeds United") 3–0 at Wembley. Villa finished fourth in the league that season, and fifth the season after.

Following a dip in form, Doug Ellis sacked Little and replaced him with another former Villa player [John Gregory](/wiki/John_Gregory_(footballer) "John Gregory (footballer)") in February 1998. One of his first matches in charge was the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/Uefa_Cup "Uefa Cup") quarter-final against [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid"), which Villa lost on away goals over two-legs. In the summer of 1998, Yorke was transferred to Manchester United for £12.6 million. Gregory managed four top-eight finishes in the league and took the club to an FA Cup final in 2000 with a side that included [David James](/wiki/David_James_(footballer,_born_1970) "David James (footballer, born 1970)"), [Dion Dublin](/wiki/Dion_Dublin "Dion Dublin"), [Paul Merson](/wiki/Paul_Merson "Paul Merson") and [Gareth Barry](/wiki/Gareth_Barry "Gareth Barry") but was unable to assemble a team capable of challenging for Champions League places. At the end of the season Villa's captain [Gareth Southgate](/wiki/Gareth_Southgate "Gareth Southgate") handed in a transfer request, claiming that "if I am to achieve in my career, it is time to move on".[[52]](#cite_note-52) Gregory's frustration at the lack of investment in the team led to him publicly accusing Ellis of being "stuck a time warp"; their relationship remained strained until Gregory resigned in January 2002.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Ellis appointed [Graham Taylor](/wiki/Graham_Taylor "Graham Taylor") for a second spell in February 2002, but a 16th-place finish in the league led to his replacement with [David O'Leary](/wiki/David_O%27Leary "David O'Leary") in June 2003. After a sixth-place finish in his first season, Villa the finished 10th and 16th, leading to O'Leary leaving in the summer of 2006.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Gareth_Barry_Aston_Villa-FH_414.jpg/250px-Gareth_Barry_Aston_Villa-FH_414.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gareth_Barry_Aston_Villa-FH_414.jpg)

Gareth Barry is Villa's record appearance holder in the Premier League.

After 23 years as chairman and single biggest shareholder (approximately 38%), Ellis sold his stake in Aston Villa due to ill-health at the age of 82. American businessman [Randy Lerner](/wiki/Randy_Lerner "Randy Lerner"), owner of [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") franchise the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"), completed his takeover in September 2006.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The arrival of a new owner in Lerner and of manager [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill") marked the start of a new period of optimism at Villa Park and sweeping changes occurred throughout the club including a new badge, investment in state-of-the-art facilities at the [Bodymoor Heath Training Ground](/wiki/Bodymoor_Heath_Training_Ground "Bodymoor Heath Training Ground") and significant investment in the squad in the summer of 2007.[[57]](#cite_note-new_badge-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) The first Cup final of the Lerner era came in 2010 when Villa were beaten 2–1 in the [League Cup Final](/wiki/2010_Football_League_Cup_Final "2010 Football League Cup Final").[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Just five days before the opening day of the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_in_English_football "2010–11 in English football"), O'Neill resigned as manager, despite three consecutive 6th-place finishes, due to frustration in the lack of investment in the squad, following the sale of star players [Gareth Barry](/wiki/Gareth_Barry "Gareth Barry"), [James Milner](/wiki/James_Milner "James Milner") and [Ashley Young](/wiki/Ashley_Young "Ashley Young").[[60]](#cite_note-ONeill_Leaves-60) His replacement [Gérard Houllier](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Houllier "Gérard Houllier") stepped down due to ill-health in September 2011, to be replaced by [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City "Birmingham City") manager [Alex McLeish](/wiki/Alex_McLeish "Alex McLeish"), despite fan protests against his appointment.[[61]](#cite_note-61) McLeish's contract was terminated at the end of the 2011–12 season after Villa finished in 16th place,[[62]](#cite_note-62) and he was replaced by [Paul Lambert](/wiki/Paul_Lambert "Paul Lambert").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In February 2012, the club announced a financial loss of £53.9 million,[[64]](#cite_note-64) and Lerner put the club up for sale three months later.[[65]](#cite_note-65) With Lerner still on board, but unwilling to spend following the stock market crash of 2008, the club was uncompetitive for several seasons, culminating in the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Aston_Villa_F.C._season "2014–15 Aston Villa F.C. season"), when Lambert was sacked in February 2015 after the team managed just 12 goals in the first 25 league games, the lowest in Premier League history.[[66]](#cite_note-66) [Tim Sherwood](/wiki/Tim_Sherwood "Tim Sherwood") succeeded him,[[67]](#cite_note-67) and steered the club away from relegation while also leading them to the [2015 FA Cup Final](/wiki/2015_FA_Cup_Final "2015 FA Cup Final"). However, the club sold two of its star players [Christian Benteke](/wiki/Christian_Benteke "Christian Benteke") and captain [Fabian Delph](/wiki/Fabian_Delph "Fabian Delph") in the summer transfer window and did not adequately replace them. Villa struggled in the 2015–16 season, and Sherwood was sacked following six consecutive defeats.[[68]](#cite_note-68) He was replaced by [Rémi Garde](/wiki/R%C3%A9mi_Garde "Rémi Garde"), who left after just five months with Villa lying bottom of the table; his reign included a club-record 19 game winless run. The club was relegated at the end of the season, ending their 29-year stay in the top flight.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Championship to Champions League (2016–present)

In June 2016, Chinese businessman [Tony Xia](/wiki/Tony_Xia "Tony Xia") bought the club for £76 million.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Former Chelsea boss [Roberto Di Matteo](/wiki/Roberto_Di_Matteo "Roberto Di Matteo") was appointed as the club's new manager, but was dismissed after just 12 games following a poor start to the season.[[71]](#cite_note-71) He was replaced by former Birmingham manager [Steve Bruce](/wiki/Steve_Bruce "Steve Bruce").[[72]](#cite_note-72) Bruce led the team to finish fourth in the 2017–18 season, but lost in the [2018 EFL Championship play-off final](/wiki/2018_EFL_Championship_play-off_final "2018 EFL Championship play-off final") to [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.").

Following failure to secure promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), the club faced significant financial difficulties. Following rumours that [administration](/wiki/Administration_(British_football) "Administration (British football)") was imminent, Xia looked to sell the club.[[73]](#cite_note-:10-73) On 20 July 2018 it was announced that the [NSWE Group](/wiki/V_Sports "V Sports"), a consortium consisting of Egyptian billionaire [Nassef Sawiris](/wiki/Nassef_Sawiris "Nassef Sawiris") and the American billionaire [Wes Edens](/wiki/Wes_Edens "Wes Edens"), were to invest in the football club. They purchased a controlling 55% stake in the club, and Sawiris took over the role of club chairman, appointing [Christian Purslow](/wiki/Christian_Purslow "Christian Purslow") as CEO.[[74]](#cite_note-auto-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Jack_Grealish_aug_2014.jpg/250px-Jack_Grealish_aug_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Grealish_aug_2014.jpg)

In 2021, Aston Villa sold [Jack Grealish](/wiki/Jack_Grealish "Jack Grealish") to Manchester City for a British record £100 million.

In October 2018, Bruce was dismissed after winning only once in a nine match stretch.[[75]](#cite_note-75) He was replaced by [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") manager and boyhood Villa fan [Dean Smith](/wiki/Dean_Smith_(footballer,_born_1971) "Dean Smith (footballer, born 1971)"),[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) who led the team to fifth place, and reaching the playoffs again—helped on by a club-record 10 league game winning streak. They reached the [2019 EFL Championship play-off final](/wiki/2019_EFL_Championship_play-off_final "2019 EFL Championship play-off final") and defeated [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") 2–1 to gain promotion back to the Premier League after a three-year absence.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

On the eve of Villa's Premier League return, [Recon Group](/wiki/Recon_Group "Recon Group")'s minority share ownership was bought out by NSWE, meaning Xia no longer had any stake in the club.[[79]](#cite_note-:0-79) Villa's first season back in the Premier League saw a significant overhaul of the squad, with 12 players signed during the summer transfer window. The team battled relegation for most of the season, but stayed up on the final day with a 17th-place finish.[[80]](#cite_note-80) In Villa's second season back in the Premier League, Smith oversaw an 11th-place finish, but was unable to persuade star player and captain [Jack Grealish](/wiki/Jack_Grealish "Jack Grealish") to remain at the club after Manchester City's British-record £100 million bid triggered his release clause. Following a poor start to the 2021–22 season, which saw seven losses in the club's opening 11 games, Dean Smith was dismissed.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Aston Villa appointed former [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") and England captain [Steven Gerrard](/wiki/Steven_Gerrard "Steven Gerrard") as head coach on 11 November 2021.[[82]](#cite_note-82) After a poor start to the 2022–23 season, in which Villa won just twice and scored only seven goals in their opening 11 games, Gerrard was sacked in October 2022,[[83]](#cite_note-83) and replaced by four-time [Europa League](/wiki/Europa_League "Europa League")-winning Spanish manager [Unai Emery](/wiki/Unai_Emery "Unai Emery").[[84]](#cite_note-84) He led Villa to 7th place and qualification for the [Europa Conference League](/wiki/Europa_Conference_League "Europa Conference League") in his first season.[[85]](#cite_note-85) In the 2023–24 season, Emery led the club to the [Conference League semi-finals](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_UEFA_Europa_Conference_League "2023–24 UEFA Europa Conference League"),[[86]](#cite_note-86) where they were knocked out by [Olympiacos](/wiki/Olympiacos_F.C. "Olympiacos F.C."), and a fourth-place finish in the league, securing participation in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for the first time since 1982–83.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In the following [Champions League campaign](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Champions_League "2024–25 UEFA Champions League"), the team reached the quarter-finals before a 5–4 aggregate loss to eventual winners [Paris Saint-Germain](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_FC "Paris Saint-Germain FC").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

In February 2026, Aston Villa were reported to be facing a second fine from UEFA for breaching its financial regulations by exceeding the squad cost limits for the 2025 season.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

## Colours and badge

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Aston Villa F.C. kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C._kits "commons:Aston Villa F.C. kits").

![Football kit (white jersey with two broad, red, vertical stripes; red shorts with side-stripes; and white socks)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Kit_left_arm.svg/40px-Kit_left_arm.svg.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/86/Kit_body_lightbluehalf.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Kit_body.svg/40px-Kit_body.svg.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Kit_right_arm.svg/40px-Kit_right_arm.svg.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/Kit_shorts_adidaswhite.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Kit_shorts.svg/120px-Kit_shorts.svg.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Kit_socks_long.svg/120px-Kit_socks_long.svg.png)

Villa's proposed kit of 1886[[90]](#cite_note-histkit-90)

The club colours are a claret shirt with sky blue sleeves, white shorts with claret and blue trim, and sky blue socks with claret and white trim. They were the original wearers of the claret and blue. Villa's colours at the outset were royal blue caps and stockings, royal blue and scarlet "striped" (in the context of the time, hooped) jerseys, and white knickerbockers, one of the club rules including a provision that "no member can take part in a match without wearing the above uniform".[[91]](#cite_note-91) For a few years after that (1877–79) the team wore several different kits from all white, blue and black, red and blue to plain green. By 1880, black jerseys with a [Scottish Lion Rampant](/wiki/Royal_Banner_of_Scotland "Royal Banner of Scotland") embroidered on the chest were introduced by Villa's Scottish leaders [William McGregor](/wiki/William_McGregor_(football) "William McGregor (football)") and [George Ramsay](/wiki/George_Ramsay_(footballer,_born_1855) "George Ramsay (footballer, born 1855)"). This remained the first choice strip for six years. On Monday, 8 November 1886, an entry in the club's official minute book states:

> (i) Proposed and seconded that the colours be chocolate and sky blue shirts and that we order two dozen.
> (ii) Proposed and seconded that Mr McGregor be requested to supply them at the lowest quotation.

The chocolate colour later became claret.[[90]](#cite_note-histkit-90) Nobody is quite sure why claret and blue became the club's adopted colours.[[90]](#cite_note-histkit-90) Several other football teams adopted their colours, or also play in claret and blue, including [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."), [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."), [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") and Turkish club [Trabzonspor](/wiki/Trabzonspor "Trabzonspor").[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_FC "Crystal Palace FC") also played in Villa's colours until the 1970s.

A new badge was revealed in May 2007, for the [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_English_football "2007–08 in English football") season and beyond. The new badge includes a [star](/wiki/Star_(football_badge) "Star (football badge)") to represent the European Cup win in 1982, and has a light blue background behind Villa's 'lion rampant'. The traditional motto *"Prepared"* remains in the badge, and the name Aston Villa has been shortened to AVFC, FC having been omitted from the previous badge. The lion is now unified as opposed to fragmented lions of the past. [Randy Lerner](/wiki/Randy_Lerner "Randy Lerner") petitioned fans to help with the design of the new badge.[[57]](#cite_note-new_badge-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/86/Aston_Villa_F.C._logo.svg/250px-Aston_Villa_F.C._logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Aston_Villa_F.C._logo.svg)

Kit-exclusive badge for the 2023–24 season.

On 6 April 2016, the club confirmed that it would be using a new badge from the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Premier_League "2016–17 Premier League") after consulting fan groups for suggestions. The lion in the new badge has claws added to it, and the word "Prepared" was removed to increase the size of the lion and club initials in the badge.[[11]](#cite_note-introducing-11) In November 2022, following a fan-led vote, the club announced it would adopt a new badge for the following season.[[94]](#cite_note-94) The new badge's usage was later clarified to be exclusive to on-field kits and training wear by club president of business operations, Chris Heck, with the existing badge continuing to be utilized as the primary in all other channels.[[95]](#cite_note-95) In May 2024, the club reverted to a shield-shaped badge.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Aston Villa's kit was produced by local manufacturers until 1974, when [Umbro](/wiki/Umbro "Umbro") became the first kit supplier to have its logo on a Villa shirt. Aston Villa's first shirt sponsor was Davenports Breweries in the 1982–83 season.[[90]](#cite_note-histkit-90) Aston Villa forwent commercial kit sponsorship for the 2008–09 and 2009–10 seasons; instead advertising the charity [Acorns Children's Hospice](/wiki/Acorns_Children%27s_Hospice "Acorns Children's Hospice"), the first deal of its kind in Premier League history.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The partnership continued until 2010 when a commercial sponsor replaced Acorns, with the hospice becoming the club's Official Charity Partner.[[102]](#cite_note-102) A shirt sleeve sponsor was used for the first time in the 2019–20 season with BR88 being displayed.[[103]](#cite_note-103) In August 2025, Aston Villa announced a partnership with Red Bull, making the company the club's Official Energy Drink Partner.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)

## Stadium

Main article: [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/VillaPark_PanoramaFromTrinityRoadStand.jpg/960px-VillaPark_PanoramaFromTrinityRoadStand.jpg)](/wiki/File:VillaPark_PanoramaFromTrinityRoadStand.jpg "A panorama of Villa Park from the Trinity Road Stand, showing from left to right the North Stand, the Doug Ellis Stand and the Holte End")

A panorama of Villa Park from the Trinity Road Stand, showing from left to right the North Stand, the Doug Ellis Stand and the Holte End

Aston Villa's current home venue is Villa Park; the team previously played at Aston Park (1874–1876) and [Wellington Road](/wiki/Wellington_Road_(Perry_Barr) "Wellington Road (Perry Barr)") (1876–1897). Villa Park is the largest football stadium in the English Midlands, and the eighth largest stadium in England. It has hosted 16 England internationals at senior level, the first in 1899, and the most recent in 2005. Thus, it was the first English ground to stage international football in three different centuries.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Villa Park is the most used stadium in FA Cup semi-final history, having hosted 55 semi-finals. In 2022, the club announced plans to rebuild the North Stand and part of the Trinity Road stand, which will take the maximum capacity over 50,000.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

The current training ground is located at [Bodymoor Heath](/wiki/Bodymoor_Heath_Training_Ground "Bodymoor Heath Training Ground") near [Kingsbury](/wiki/Kingsbury,_Warwickshire "Kingsbury, Warwickshire") in north [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), the site for which was purchased by former chairman Doug Ellis in the early 1970s from a local farmer. Although Bodymoor Heath was state-of-the-art in the 1970s, by the late 1990s the facilities had started to look dated. In November 2005, Ellis and Aston Villa plc announced a state of the art [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")13 million redevelopment of Bodymoor in two phases. The new training ground was officially unveiled on 6 May 2007, by then manager [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill"), then-team captain [Gareth Barry](/wiki/Gareth_Barry "Gareth Barry") and 1982 European Cup winning team captain [Dennis Mortimer](/wiki/Dennis_Mortimer "Dennis Mortimer"), with the Aston Villa squad moving in for the 2007–08 season.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

It was announced on 6 August 2014, that Villa Park would appear in the [FIFA video game](/wiki/FIFA_(video_game_series) "FIFA (video game series)") from *[FIFA 15](/wiki/FIFA_15 "FIFA 15")*, with all other Premier League stadiums also fully licensed from this game onwards.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Aston Villa operates under the guidance of the [Safety Advisory Group](/w/index.php?title=Safety_Advisory_Group_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Safety Advisory Group (United Kingdom) (page does not exist)") (SAG), which is responsible for issuing safety certificates for matches held at [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"), taking into account both physical and security considerations. In 2023, ahead of a [UEFA Europa Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_Conference_League "UEFA Europa Conference League") match against [Legia Warsaw](/wiki/Legia_Warsaw "Legia Warsaw"), away supporters were barred from attending following clashes outside the stadium.[[111]](#cite_note-espn-111) Similarly, in November 2025, fans of [Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C.](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_F.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C.") were [prohibited from attending](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_F.C.#October_2025_Aston_Villa_ban_of_supporters "Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C.") a scheduled [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") match at Villa Park mainly due to the threat of violence by Maccabi fans.[[112]](#cite_note-reuters-112)[[113]](#cite_note-guardian-20251124-113)[[114]](#cite_note-wmp-20251121-114)

## Ownership

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Lerner%2C_Randy.jpg/250px-Lerner%2C_Randy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lerner,_Randy.jpg)

[Randy Lerner](/wiki/Randy_Lerner "Randy Lerner"), the club owner of Aston Villa (2006–2016)

See also: [V Sports](/wiki/V_Sports "V Sports")

The first shares in the club were issued towards the end of the 19th century as a result of legislation that was intended to codify the growing numbers of professional teams and players in the Association Football leagues. FA teams were required to distribute shares to investors as a way of facilitating trading among the teams without implicating the FA itself. This trading continued for much of the 20th century until Ellis started buying up many of the shares in the 1960s. He was chairman and substantial shareholder of "Aston Villa F.C." from 1968 to 1975 and the majority shareholder from 1982 to 2006. The club were [floated](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") on the [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange") (LSE) in 1996, and the share price fluctuated in the ten years after the flotation.[[115]](#cite_note-115) In 2006, it was announced that several consortia and individuals were considering bids for Aston Villa.[[116]](#cite_note-EndEllisEra-116)

On 14 August 2006, it was confirmed that [Randy Lerner](/wiki/Randy_Lerner "Randy Lerner"), then owner of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"), had reached an agreement of £62.6 million with Aston Villa for a takeover of the club. Lerner took full control on 18 September with Ellis and his board replaced with a new board by Lerner on 19 September 2006.[[116]](#cite_note-EndEllisEra-116) Lerner appointed himself chairman of the club with [Charles Krulak](/wiki/Charles_Krulak "Charles Krulak") as a non-executive director and Ellis awarded the honorary position of Chairman Emeritus.[[117]](#cite_note-Ellisearner-117) Lerner put the club up for sale in May 2014, valuing it at an estimated £200 million.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

On 18 May 2016, Randy Lerner agreed the sale of Aston Villa to [Recon Group](/wiki/Recon_Group "Recon Group"), owned by Chinese businessman [Tony Xia](/wiki/Tony_Xia "Tony Xia"). The sale was completed on 14 June 2016 for a reported £76 million, after being approved by the Football League.[[119]](#cite_note-SaleCompleteBBC-119) After a period of high expenditure and failing to secure promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") in the [2017–18 Championship](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_EFL_Championship "2017–18 EFL Championship") playoff final, financial difficulties at the club began to mount. Amid rumours that Villa risked entering [administration](/wiki/Administration_(British_football) "Administration (British football)"), Xia entered talks to sell the club in June 2018.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[73]](#cite_note-:10-73)

On 20 July 2018, it was announced that [V Sports](/wiki/V_Sports "V Sports") (at the time known as NSWE Group), a consortium consisting of Egyptian billionaire [Nassef Sawiris](/wiki/Nassef_Sawiris "Nassef Sawiris") and the American billionaire [Wes Edens](/wiki/Wes_Edens "Wes Edens"), were to invest in the football club. They purchased a controlling 55% stake in the club, and Sawiris took over the role of club chairman.[[74]](#cite_note-auto-74) Prior to V Sports purchasing the club in 2018, it was reported that [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") minority owner, [Peter Freund](/wiki/Peter_Freund "Peter Freund") was interested in purchasing the club for £75 million.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) On 9 August 2019, following Villa's promotion to the Premier League, [Companies House](/wiki/Companies_House "Companies House") revealed that the remainder of Xia's shares had been bought out, and that Xia no longer had any stake in the club.[[79]](#cite_note-:0-79)

On 15 December 2023, Aston Villa announced that [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast")-backed American investment firm Atairos would invest in V Sports, the ultimate parent company of the club.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The agreement, which saw Atairos obtain a stake of approximately 20% in V Sports, valued Villa at more than £500 million.[[124]](#cite_note-124) The deal with Atairos was finalised on 12 April 2024, with Atairos CEO and former [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") CFO Michael Angelakis appointed to Villa's board of directors.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)

## Social responsibility

Aston Villa has a unique relationship with the [Acorns Children's Hospice](/wiki/Acorns_Children%27s_Hospice "Acorns Children's Hospice") charity that is groundbreaking in English football.[[127]](#cite_note-127) In a first for the Premier League, Aston Villa donated the front of its kit shirts, usually reserved for high-paying sponsorships, to Acorns Hospice so that the charity would gain significant additional visibility and more funds.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Outside of the shirt sponsorship the club has paid for hospice care for the charity as well as regularly providing player visits to hospice locations.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In September 2010, Aston Villa launched an initiative at Villa Park called Villa Midlands Food (VMF) where the club will spend two years training students with Aston Villa hospitality and events in association with Birmingham City Council. The club opened a restaurant in the Trinity Road Stand staffed with 12 students recruited from within a 10-mile (16 km) radius of Villa Park with most of the food served in the restaurant sourced locally.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Aston Villa Foundation

In 2016, Aston Villa created a [registered charity](/wiki/Registered_Charity "Registered Charity"), the Aston Villa Foundation.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The aim of the charity is to deliver the social responsibility work of Aston Villa. Working alongside key local and national stakeholders, the Foundation delivers projects such as football in the community, disability, health and wellbeing, education, interventions and community relations.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

In May 2021, [Prince William, Duke of Cambridge](/wiki/Prince_William,_Duke_of_Cambridge "Prince William, Duke of Cambridge"), met with members of the Foundation at Aston Villa's [Bodymoor Heath Training Ground](/wiki/Bodymoor_Heath_Training_Ground "Bodymoor Heath Training Ground"). This was following the Foundation providing 1000 hot meals a week to local organisations during the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), as well as allowing a local [NHS Trust](/wiki/NHS_trust "NHS trust") to make use of [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park")'s facilities.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

## Supporters and rivalries

See also: [Second City derby](/wiki/Second_City_derby "Second City derby") and [Aston Villa F.C.–West Bromwich Albion F.C. rivalry](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C.%E2%80%93West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C._rivalry "Aston Villa F.C.–West Bromwich Albion F.C. rivalry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/12th_Man.jpg/250px-12th_Man.jpg)](/wiki/File:12th_Man.jpg)

Aston Villa fans in Villa Park's Holte End, proclaiming themselves to be the team's [12th man](/wiki/12th_man_(football) "12th man (football)")

Aston Villa have a large fanbase and draw support from all over the [Midlands](/wiki/English_Midlands "English Midlands") and beyond, with supporters' clubs all across the world. Former Villa chief executive Richard Fitzgerald has stated that the ethnicity of the supporters is currently 98% white. When Randy Lerner's regime took over at Villa Park, they aimed to improve the support from ethnic minorities. A number of organisations have been set up to support the local community, including Aston Pride.[[135]](#cite_note-135) A Villa in the Community programme has also been set up to encourage support among young people in the region.[[136]](#cite_note-136) The new owners have also initiated several surveys aimed at gaining the opinions of Villa fans and to involve them in the decision-making process. Meetings also occur every three months where supporters are invited by ballot and are invited to ask questions to the board.[[137]](#cite_note-137) In 2011, the club supported a supporter-based initiative for an official anthem to boost the atmosphere at Villa Park. The song "The Bells Are Ringing" is to be played before games.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

Like many English football clubs, Aston Villa has had several [hooligan firms](/wiki/Football_hooliganism "Football hooliganism") associated with it: Villa Youth, Steamers, [Villa Hardcore](/wiki/Aston_Villa_Hardcore "Aston Villa Hardcore") and the C-Crew, the last mentioned being very active during the 1970s and 1980s. In 2004, several Villa firms were involved in a fight with [QPR](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.") fans outside Villa Park in which a steward died.[[139]](#cite_note-139) As can be seen across the whole of English football, due to incidents like the aforementioned, the hooligan groups have now been marginalised.[[140]](#cite_note-140) The main groupings of supporters can now be found in a number of domestic and international supporters' clubs. This includes the Official Aston Villa Supporters Club which also has many smaller regional and international sections.[[141]](#cite_note-141) There were several independent supporters clubs during the reign of Doug Ellis but most of these disbanded after his retirement.[[117]](#cite_note-Ellisearner-117) The supporter group [My Old Man Said](/wiki/My_Old_Man_Said "My Old Man Said") formed to stand up for Villa supporters' rights, as a direct result of Villa supporters' protest against the club's appointment of [Alex McLeish](/wiki/Alex_McLeish "Alex McLeish"). The club's supporters also publish [fanzines](/wiki/Fanzine "Fanzine") such as *Heroes and Villains* and *The Holy Trinity*.

Aston Villa's arch-rivals are Birmingham City, with games between the two clubs known as the [Second City Derby](/wiki/Second_City_Derby "Second City Derby").[[9]](#cite_note-Derby-9) Historically though, [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") have arguably been Villa's greatest rivals, a view highlighted in a fan survey, conducted in 2003.[[142]](#cite_note-142) The two teams contested three FA Cup finals in the late 19th century. Villa also enjoy less heated local rivalries with [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."). Through the relegation of West Brom and Birmingham City, to the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), in the [2005–06](/wiki/FA_Premier_League_2005%E2%80%9306 "FA Premier League 2005–06") season, at the start of [2006–07](/wiki/FA_Premier_League_2006%E2%80%9307 "FA Premier League 2006–07") Premiership season, Villa were the only Midlands club in that League. The nearest opposing team Villa faced during that season was [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C."), who played 62 miles (100 km) away in South Yorkshire.[[143]](#cite_note-143) For the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Premier_League "2010–11 Premier League"), West Bromwich Albion were promoted and joined Aston Villa, Wolverhampton Wanderers, and Birmingham City in the Premier League. This marked the first time that the "West Midlands' Big Four" clubs were in the Premier League at the same time, and the first time together in the top flight since the [1983–84 season](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_in_English_football "1983–84 in English football"). Birmingham were relegated at the end of the 2010–11 season, ending this period.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

The rivalry with Birmingham City was renewed in 2016–17 when Aston Villa suffered relegation from the Premier League.[[145]](#cite_note-145) They were joined by West Brom two years later,[[146]](#cite_note-146) but Villa won promotion back to the Premier League in 2019.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

In 2024, a supporter group named 1897 Group – named after the year that the club first used Villa Park as their home stadium – formed with the stated intention of vocally and visually enhancing the atmosphere for the club's matches at Villa Park. Following a subsequent meeting with club officials, 1897 Group were allocated a section of seats in the stadium's Doug Ellis Stand for a Premier League match against [A.F.C. Bournemouth](/wiki/A.F.C._Bournemouth "A.F.C. Bournemouth"). This launched the group's physical presence at matches.[[148]](#cite_note-148)
1897 Group also help to organise and carry out flag and [tifo](/wiki/Tifo "Tifo") displays in Villa Park's Holte End stand and also in other sections of the stadium for some Villa home matches. Additionally, they have also organised and promoted Villa fan marches where Villa supporters are encouraged by the group to congregate at a designated point in the town or city that Villa are playing in and walk all together to the stadium.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)

## Records and statistics

Main article: [List of Aston Villa F.C. records and statistics](/wiki/List_of_Aston_Villa_F.C._records_and_statistics "List of Aston Villa F.C. records and statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/AstonVillaFC_League_Performance.svg/250px-AstonVillaFC_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:AstonVillaFC_League_Performance.svg)

Chart showing the progress of Aston Villa F.C. through the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system")

The 2024–25 campaign is Aston Villa's 111th season in the top tier of English football. The only club to have spent longer in the top flight are [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C."), with 122 seasons,[[151]](#cite_note-151) making Aston Villa versus Everton the most-played fixture in English top-flight football. Aston Villa was relegated from the top tier of English football in 2016, having played in every Premier League season since its establishment in [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_FA_Premier_League "1992–93 FA Premier League"), but was promoted back in [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_EFL_Championship "2018–19 EFL Championship"). They are ninth in the [All-time Premier League table](/wiki/All-time_Premier_League_table "All-time Premier League table"), and have the fifth-highest total of major honours (20) won by an English club.[[152]](#cite_note-alltimetable-152)

Aston Villa currently holds the record number of league goals scored by any team in the English top flight; 128 goals were scored in the [1930–31 season](/wiki/1930%E2%80%9331_Football_League "1930–31 Football League"), one more than [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") who won the league that season for the first time, with Villa runners-up.[[153]](#cite_note-153) Villa forward [Archie Hunter](/wiki/Archie_Hunter "Archie Hunter") became the first player to score in every round of the FA Cup in Villa's victorious 1887 campaign. Villa's longest unbeaten home run in the FA Cup spanned 13 years and 19 games, from 1888 to 1901.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

Aston Villa are one of six English teams to have won the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"). They did so on 26 May 1982 in [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), beating [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") 1–0 thanks to [Peter Withe](/wiki/Peter_Withe "Peter Withe")'s goal.[[155]](#cite_note-155) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Aston_Villa_F.C.&action=edit), Aston Villa are only surpassed by Tottenham Hotspur (79), for providing the most [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") internationals with 77 Villa players debuting for England since 1882.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)

## Honours

See also: [List of Aston Villa F.C. records and statistics](/wiki/List_of_Aston_Villa_F.C._records_and_statistics "List of Aston Villa F.C. records and statistics") and [List of Aston Villa F.C. seasons](/wiki/List_of_Aston_Villa_F.C._seasons "List of Aston Villa F.C. seasons")

Aston Villa have won both European and domestic league honours. The club's last English honour was in 1996 when they won the [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup"), and most recently they won the [2001 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2001_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2001 UEFA Intertoto Cup").

Captain [John Devey](/wiki/John_Devey "John Devey") standing in front of the FA Cup which the club won for the second time in 1895

### Club captains

Since the foundation of the club in 1874, forty five players have been club captain of Aston Villa. [John Devey](/wiki/Jack_Devey "Jack Devey") who was captain from 1891 to 1898, is their most successful captain, leading Villa to five league titles and two FA Cup triumphs during the club's 'Golden Age'. The present captain is Scotland international [John McGinn](/wiki/John_McGinn "John McGinn").
