[Andy Gray](/wiki/Andy_Gray_(footballer,_born_1955) "Andy Gray (footballer, born 1955)") was voted the [PFA Players' Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Players%27_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Players' Player of the Year") and [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year") in 1977.

There have been many players who can be called notable throughout Aston Villa's history. These can be classified and recorded in several forms. The Halls of Fame and PFA Players of the Year are noted below. Aston Villa have had several players who were [one-club men](/wiki/List_of_one-club_men_in_association_football "List of one-club men in association football"), including inaugural club Hall of Fame inductee [Billy Walker](/wiki/Billy_Walker_(footballer,_born_1897) "Billy Walker (footballer, born 1897)"). In 1998, to celebrate the 100th season of League football, [The Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") released a list entitled the [Football League 100 Legends](/wiki/Football_League_100_Legends "Football League 100 Legends") that consisted of "100 legendary [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") players". There were seven players included on the list who played for Villa: [Danny Blanchflower](/wiki/Danny_Blanchflower "Danny Blanchflower"), [Trevor Ford](/wiki/Trevor_Ford "Trevor Ford"), [Archie Hunter](/wiki/Archie_Hunter "Archie Hunter"), [Sam Hardy](/wiki/Sam_Hardy_(footballer) "Sam Hardy (footballer)"), [Paul McGrath](/wiki/Paul_McGrath_(footballer) "Paul McGrath (footballer)"), [Clem Stephenson](/wiki/Clem_Stephenson "Clem Stephenson") and [Peter Schmeichel](/wiki/Peter_Schmeichel "Peter Schmeichel") (who went on to play for Villa three seasons later).[[180]](#cite_note-182) Schmeichel was later inducted into the [Premier League Hall of Fame](/wiki/Premier_League_Hall_of_Fame "Premier League Hall of Fame") in 2022, with former Villa captain and coach [John Terry](/wiki/John_Terry "John Terry") inducted in 2024.[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184)

Aston Villa have had a number of players who have been successful on the international stage while they were at the club. Paul McGrath and [Steve Staunton](/wiki/Steve_Staunton "Steve Staunton") (Republic of Ireland), as well as [Olof Mellberg](/wiki/Olof_Mellberg "Olof Mellberg") (Sweden) all captained their national sides in the 1990, 2002 and 2006 World Cups respectively.[[183]](#cite_note-185)[[184]](#cite_note-PMG-186) McGrath appeared nine times at the World Cup while at Aston Villa, a record for an active Villa player.[[184]](#cite_note-PMG-186) Emiliano Martínez has won all his senior [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") caps to date while at Aston Villa, having debuted in June 2021. He is currently the most decorated international player for Aston Villa having won the [2021 Copa América](/wiki/2021_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2021 Copa América"), the [2022 Finalissima](/wiki/2022_Finalissima "2022 Finalissima"), the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup"), and the [2024 Copa América](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2024 Copa América"); he was awarded the *Golden Glove* awards at the 2021 and 2024 Copa América and the 2022 World Cup; he was twice named both the [Best FIFA Goalkeeper](/wiki/The_Best_FIFA_Goalkeeper "The Best FIFA Goalkeeper") and the [Yashin Trophy](/wiki/2023_Ballon_d%27Or "2023 Ballon d'Or") winner between 2022 and 2024.[[185]](#cite_note-Copa-187)[[186]](#cite_note-WCGG-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-190)[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192)

Three Aston Villa players have won the [PFA Players' Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Players%27_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Players' Player of the Year") award. In [1977](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_in_English_football "1976–77 in English football") [Andy Gray](/wiki/Andy_Gray_(footballer_born_1955) "Andy Gray (footballer born 1955)") won the award. In [1990](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_in_English_football "1989–90 in English football") it was awarded to [David Platt](/wiki/David_Platt_(footballer) "David Platt (footballer)"), whilst Paul McGrath won it in [1993](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_in_English_football "1992–93 in English football"). The [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year"), which is awarded to players under the age of 23, has been awarded to five players from Aston Villa: Andy Gray in [1977](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_in_English_football "1976–77 in English football"); [Gary Shaw](/wiki/Gary_Shaw_(footballer,_born_1961) "Gary Shaw (footballer, born 1961)") in [1981](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_in_English_football "1980–81 in English football"); [Ashley Young](/wiki/Ashley_Young "Ashley Young") in [2009](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_in_English_football "2008–09 in English football"), [James Milner](/wiki/James_Milner "James Milner") in [2010](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_in_English_football "2009–10 in English football") and [Morgan Rogers](/wiki/Morgan_Rogers "Morgan Rogers") in [2025](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_in_English_football "2024–25 in English football").

The [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum") administers the [English Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/English_Football_Hall_of_Fame "English Football Hall of Fame") which currently contains one Villa team, five Villa players and one manager. The 1982 European Cup-winning team were inducted into the Hall of Fame in October 2011.[[191]](#cite_note-193) Former Aston Villa players named in the Hall of Fame are Clem Stephenson, Danny Blanchflower, Peter Schmeichel, [Cyrille Regis](/wiki/Cyrille_Regis "Cyrille Regis"), and Paul McGrath; as well as former manager [Joe Mercer](/wiki/Joe_Mercer "Joe Mercer").

In 2006, the club announced the creation of an Aston Villa Hall of Fame. This was voted for by fans and the inaugural induction saw 12 former players, managers and directors named.[[22]](#cite_note-Hall_of_Fame-22) Former club captain [Stiliyan Petrov](/wiki/Stiliyan_Petrov "Stiliyan Petrov") was added to the list in May 2013.[[192]](#cite_note-:1-194)

## In popular culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Thomas_Hemy_Sunderland_v_Aston_Villa_1895_A_Corner_Kick.jpg/250px-Thomas_Hemy_Sunderland_v_Aston_Villa_1895_A_Corner_Kick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Hemy_Sunderland_v_Aston_Villa_1895_A_Corner_Kick.jpg)

One of the earliest football paintings in the world, Thomas MM Hemy's "[Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_AFC "Sunderland AFC") v. Aston Villa 1895", depicts a match between the two most successful English teams of the decade.

An Aston Villa team were the subject, together with that of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."), of one of the earliest football paintings in the world – possibly the earliest – when in 1895 the artist Thomas M. M. Hemy painted a picture of a game between the teams at Sunderland's then ground [Newcastle Road](/wiki/Newcastle_Road "Newcastle Road").[[225]](#cite_note-229)

A number of television programmes have included references to Aston Villa over the past few decades. In the sitcom *[Porridge](/wiki/Porridge_(1974_TV_series) "Porridge (1974 TV series)")*, the character [Lennie Godber](/wiki/Lennie_Godber "Lennie Godber") is a Villa supporter.[[226]](#cite_note-230) When filming began on *[Dad's Army](/wiki/Dad%27s_Army "Dad's Army")*, Villa fan [Ian Lavender](/wiki/Ian_Lavender "Ian Lavender") was allowed to choose [Frank Pike](/wiki/Private_Pike "Private Pike")'s scarf from an array in the BBC wardrobe; he chose a claret and blue one – Aston Villa's colours.[[227]](#cite_note-231) The character Nessa in the BBC sitcom *[Gavin & Stacey](/wiki/Gavin_%26_Stacey "Gavin & Stacey")* was revealed as an Aston Villa fan in an episode screened in December 2009.[[228]](#cite_note-232)

In the 1952 film *[The Card](/wiki/The_Card_(1952_film) "The Card (1952 film)")*, the main character Denry Machin ([Alec Guinness](/wiki/Alec_Guinness "Alec Guinness")) becomes a town councillor and purchases the rights to locally born Aston Villa player 'Callear', the "greatest centre-forward in England", for the failing local football club.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

Villa have also featured on several occasions in prose. Stanley Woolley, a character in [Derek Robinson](/wiki/Derek_Robinson_(novelist) "Derek Robinson (novelist)")'s Booker shortlisted novel *Goshawk Squadron* is an Aston Villa fan and names a pre-war starting eleven Villa side. Together with [The Oval](/wiki/The_Oval "The Oval"), Villa Park is referenced by the poet [Philip Larkin](/wiki/Philip_Larkin "Philip Larkin") in his poem about the First World War, MCMXIV.[[230]](#cite_note-234) Aston Villa are also mentioned in Harold Pinter's play *[The Dumb Waiter](/wiki/The_Dumb_Waiter "The Dumb Waiter")*.[[231]](#cite_note-235)
The club receive a passing mention in [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley")'s debut novel *[Crome Yellow](/wiki/Crome_Yellow "Crome Yellow")*.

Notable supporters of Aston Villa include [Prince William](/wiki/Prince_William,_Duke_of_Cambridge "Prince William, Duke of Cambridge"),[[232]](#cite_note-236) former Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron"), musicians [Ozzy Osbourne](/wiki/Ozzy_Osbourne "Ozzy Osbourne") and [Geezer Butler](/wiki/Geezer_Butler "Geezer Butler") of [Black Sabbath](/wiki/Black_Sabbath "Black Sabbath"), actor [Tom Hanks](/wiki/Tom_Hanks "Tom Hanks"), violinist [Nigel Kennedy](/wiki/Nigel_Kennedy "Nigel Kennedy"), golfer [Justin Rose](/wiki/Justin_Rose "Justin Rose"), and [Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)") actors [David Bradley](/wiki/David_Bradley_(English_actor) "David Bradley (English actor)") and [Oliver Phelps](/wiki/Oliver_Phelps_(actor) "Oliver Phelps (actor)").[[233]](#cite_note-237)

## Aston Villa Women

Main article: [Aston Villa W.F.C.](/wiki/Aston_Villa_W.F.C. "Aston Villa W.F.C.")

Aston Villa have a women's football side that compete in the [Women's Super League](/wiki/Women%27s_Super_League "Women's Super League") having been promoted as champions of the [2019–20 FA Women's Championship](/wiki/2019-20_FA_Women%27s_Championship "2019-20 FA Women's Championship"). They were founded as Solihull F.C. in 1973 and affiliated to Aston Villa in 1989.[[234]](#cite_note-238)
